title: Covariance Regularisation
text: Inverse-regression methods commonly require covariance standardisation. predictors strongly collinear predictor dimension small relative sample size, sample covariance matrix may poorly conditioned singular. Regularisation changes bias-variance trade-obtain stable inverse. risdr includes sample covariance, ridge shrinkage, OAS (Chen et al. 2010), LW (Ledoit Wolf 2004), MEC.

title: Covariance Regularisation
text: Condition number effective rank describe numerical behaviour, predictive adequacy. interpreted alongside downstream subspace metrics.
code: library(risdr)  sim <- simulate_risdr_data(   n = 100,   p = 40,   d = 2,   rho = 0.9,   seed = 7001 )  estimates <- list(   sample = cov_sample(sim$X),   ridge = cov_ridge(sim$X, lambda = 0.10),   oas = cov_oas(sim$X),   lw = cov_lw(sim$X),   mec = cov_mec(sim$X, sim$y, nslices = 6) )  diagnostics <- do.call(   rbind,   lapply(names(estimates), function(name) {     value <- cov_diagnostics(estimates[[name]])     data.frame(       estimator = name,       minimum_eigenvalue = value$min_eigenvalue,       maximum_eigenvalue = value$max_eigenvalue,       condition_number = value$condition_number,       effective_rank = value$effective_rank     )   }) )  diagnostics #>   estimator minimum_eigenvalue maximum_eigenvalue condition_number #> 1    sample        0.013663062           13.59163         994.7718 #> 2     ridge        0.109488283           12.32966         112.6117 #> 3       oas        0.074309851           12.65522         170.3035 #> 4        lw        0.078515511           12.59984         160.4758 #> 5       mec        0.001871314           15.44685        8254.5483 #>   effective_rank #> 1             40 #> 2             40 #> 3             40 #> 4             40 #> 5             40

title: Covariance Regularisation
text: sample covariance \mathbf{S} scaled identity target \mathbf{T}, ridge estimator 𝚺̂λ=(1−λ)𝐒+λ𝐓,0≤λ≤1. \widehat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}_{\lambda} = (1-\lambda)\mathbf{S}+\lambda\mathbf{T}, \qquad 0\leq\lambda\leq 1.
code: ridge_fit <- fit_risdr(   sim$X,   sim$y,   sdr_method = "sir",   cov_method = "ridge",   d = 2,   d_max = 5,   cov_args = list(lambda = 0.15) )

title: Covariance Regularisation
text: Covariance estimation numerical stabilisation separate operations. estimated covariance may stabilised using eigenvalue floor, additive ridge term, nearest positive-definite projection. fit_risdr() interface separates controls:
code: sample_covariance <- cov_sample(sim$X)  floor_covariance <- stabilize_cov(   sample_covariance,   method = "eigenfloor",   eps = 1e-6 )  ridge_covariance <- stabilize_cov(   sample_covariance,   method = "ridge",   lambda = 1e-4 )  nearest_covariance <- stabilize_cov(   sample_covariance,   method = "nearest_pd",   keep_diag = TRUE ) separated_controls <- fit_risdr(   sim$X,   sim$y,   sdr_method = "dr",   cov_method = "ridge",   stabilization = "eigenfloor",   d = 2,   d_max = 5,   cov_args = list(lambda = 0.10),   stabilization_args = list(eps = 1e-7) )

Filtering imputation choices must estimated training data applied test data. Re-estimating preprocessing independently test data weaken interpretation external prediction.
code: library(dplyr) library(readr)  epi_data <- read_csv("data-raw/epi2024results.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)  epi_old <- epi_data |>   select(iso, country, ends_with(".old")) |>   rename_with(\(x) sub("[.]old$", "", x))  epi_new <- epi_data |>   select(iso, country, ends_with(".new")) |>   rename_with(\(x) sub("[.]new$", "", x))  X_old_full <- epi_old |> select(-iso, -country) X_new_full <- epi_new |> select(-iso, -country)  stopifnot(identical(names(X_old_full), names(X_new_full)))  missing_prop_old <- colMeans(is.na(X_old_full)) keep_names <- names(missing_prop_old[missing_prop_old < 0.40])  X_old_reduced <- X_old_full[, keep_names, drop = FALSE] X_new_reduced <- X_new_full[, keep_names, drop = FALSE]  training_variance <- vapply(X_old_reduced, var, numeric(1), na.rm = TRUE) keep_names <- names(training_variance[training_variance > 1e-6])  X_old_reduced <- X_old_reduced[, keep_names, drop = FALSE] X_new_reduced <- X_new_reduced[, keep_names, drop = FALSE]  X_old_imputed <- VIM::kNN(X_old_reduced, k = 5, imp_var = FALSE) X_new_imputed <- VIM::kNN(X_new_reduced, k = 5, imp_var = FALSE)  y_train <- X_old_imputed$EPI X_train <- X_old_imputed |> select(-EPI) y_test <- X_new_imputed$EPI X_test <- X_new_imputed |> select(-EPI)

title: risdr
text: risdr provides reproducible framework comparative sufficient dimension reduction covariance regularisation information-theoretic structural dimension selection. current modelling interface supports continuous responses. package implements: sliced inverse regression (SIR); sliced average variance estimation (SAVE); directional regression (DR); principal Hessian directions (pHd); sample, ridge, Oracle Approximating Shrinkage (OAS), Ledoit-Wolf (LW), Maximum Entropy Covariance (MEC) estimators; AIC, BIC, CAIC, ICOMP, CICOMP structural dimension criteria; V-fold, repeated, complexity-aware cross-validation; prediction diagnostics, subspace recovery measures, simulation utilities, base R plotting methods.

text: Component-specific arguments separated explicitly:
code: library(risdr)  set.seed(2026)  sim <- simulate_risdr_data(   n = 160,   p = 20,   d = 2,   rho = 0.6,   model = "linear_quadratic",   seed = 2026 )  fit <- fit_risdr(   X = sim$X,   y = sim$y,   sdr_method = "dr",   cov_method = "oas",   nslices = 6,   d_max = 6,   selector = "cicomp" )  fit summary(fit)  prediction <- predict(fit, sim$X[1:10, , drop = FALSE]) evaluate_prediction(sim$y[1:10], prediction, d = fit$d) fit_ridge <- fit_risdr(   X = sim$X,   y = sim$y,   sdr_method = "sir",   cov_method = "ridge",   d = 2,   d_max = 4,   cov_args = list(lambda = 0.15),   stabilization_args = list(eps = 1e-7),   sdr_args = list(slice_type = "quantile") )

title: Compute Directional Regression — compute_dr
text: Directional Regression computed using pairwise-slice formulation. $$ M_{DR} = \sum_h \sum_k p_h p_k (2I_p - A_{hk}) (2I_p - A_{hk})^\top $$ $$ A_{hk} = \Sigma_h + \Sigma_k + (\mu_h - \mu_k) (\mu_h - \mu_k)^\top $$
